rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PETER B. BLASKO TRUST
rulings.Rulings: [1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Respondents' unopposed request for Judicial Notice of the date of filing of, and the admissions and positions advanced in, Petitioner's verified Petition for an Order Compelling Accounting, etc., filed on November 12, 2019, and Petitioner's unopposed Request for Judicial Notice of Respondents' Trustees' Preliminary Response to Petition, etc. filed on January 24, 2020, are GRANTED. Admissions of material facts made in an opposing party's pleadings are binding on that party as "judicial admissions." They are conclusive concessions of the truth of those matters, are effectively removed as issues from the litigation, and may not be contradicted by the party whose pleadings are used against him or her. Valerio v. Andrew Youngquist Const. (2002) 103 Cal.App.4th 1264, 1271; St. Paul Mercury Ins. Co. v. Frontier Pac. Ins. Co. (2003) 111 Cal.App.4th 1234, 1248 (citing text); Uhrich v. State Farm Fire & Cas. Co. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 598, 613 (citing text); Myers v. Trendwest Resorts, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 735, 746 (citing text). Respondents' request for Judicial Notice that November 12, 2019, is 993 days after February 22, 2017, and 592 days after March 30, 2018 is GRANTED pursuant to Evidence Code Section 451(f). Respondents' Request for Judicial Notice, filed with Respondents' reply, of the date of filing of, and the admissions and positions advanced in, the parties' Joint Case Management Conference Statement, jointly filed on May 28, 2021, is DENIED. The Case Management Statement is not verified by the parties but is only signed by the attorneys. Pleadings admissions not authorized by the client should not impair the client's right of action or defense. See Blaton v. Womancare, Inc. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 396, 404. Additionally, the moving party generally may not rely on additional evidence filed with its reply papers in a motion for summary adjudication. San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308, 316.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PETER B. BLASKO TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Adjudication (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [2 of 3] Respondents' Amendment to Preliminary Response to Petition filed after this Motion for Summary Adjudication was not considered. Petitioner's relevancy objections to the evidence attached to the Declaration of Ryan J. Meckfessel in of [sic] Support Motion for Summary Adjudication are OVERRULED. "'Relevant evidence' means evidence, including evidence relevant to the credibility of a witness or hearsay declarant, having any tendency in reason to prove or disprove any disputed fact that is of consequence to the determination of the action." Evidence Code Section 210. Respondents' Motion for Summary Adjudication of the Trust Contest by Beneficiary and Petitioner JoAnne Tybinka is GRANTED. After full consideration of the evidence, the Court finds that there are no triable issues of material fact, and that Respondents are entitled to judgment as a matter of law on this cause of action because the Trust Contest is time-barred. Under Probate Code Section 21210(b) a direct trust contest includes a contest that alleges the invalidity of a protected instrument or one or more of its terms based on forgery. The handwritten amendment meets the definition of an "instrument" under Probate Code Section 45 in that it is a "writing that designates a beneficiary or makes a donative transfer of property." The Petition includes a claim that the handwritten amendment is void, a nullity, and invalid because it is a forgery. Petitioner's claim is a trust contest. See Cory v. Toscano (2009) 174 Cal.App.1039. Although the statute of limitations defense was not raised in the pleadings, a Respondent's motion for summary adjudication "necessarily includes a test of the sufficiency of the complaint" and its legal effect is the same as a demurrer or motion for judgment on the pleadings. See American Airlines, Inc. v. County of San Mateo (1996) 12 Cal.4th 1110, 1118; Prue v. Brady Co./San Diego, Inc. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 1367, 1384; Slaughter v. Legal Process & Courier Service (1984) 162 Cal.App.3d 1236, 1244. "It has long been the rule in this state that when a defendant's motion for summary judgment depends on the untenability of the plaintiff's case as pleaded and not on extrinsic evidence negating an element or proving an affirmative defense it may be treated as a common law motion for judgment on the pleadings"; Tarin v. Lind (2020) 47 Cal.App.5th 395, 402. A motion for judgment on the pleadings may be made by any party to the action or by the court sua sponte. See Camacho v. Automobile Club of Southern Calif. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1394, 1396. Since a demurrer may be sustained and a motion for judgment on the pleadings may be granted where the claim is barred by the statute of limitations, and therefore the Petitioner's pleading does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action, the Court grants this motion for summary adjudication on those grounds.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PETER B. BLASKO TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Adjudication (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [3 of 3] Petitioner has presented no legal authority that would enable a tolling of the 120-day statutory repose period under Probate Code Section 16061.8. Even if such tolling were available, Petitioner failed to file her Trust Contest within 120 days from March 30, 2021, the undisputed date she received knowledge of the contested trust amendment. This determination is based on the following evidence: the fact that Respondents served the Notification by Trustee required by Probate Code Section 16061.7 on February 22, 2017; the fact that Petitioner was aware of the handwritten amendment on March 30, 2018; the fact that the Trust Contest was filed on November 12, 2019; that fact that November 12, 2019 is 993 days after February 22, 2017; and the fact that November 12, 2019, is 592 days after March 30, 2018. THEREFORE IT IS ORDERED that the motion for summary adjudication of the Trust Contest cause of action is GRANTED, and that judgment in favor of Briana Anderson Blasko, John Pickering, and Christine Heyl Wagner and against JoAnne Tybinka (Blasko) shall be entered. Respondents shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
